questions.question: What datasets did 'An Image is Worth 16x16 Words: Transformers for Image Recognition at Scale' use?
questions.gold_answer: ImageNet, ImageNet-21k, CIFAR-100
questions.system_answer: The paper used ImageNet, ImageNet-21k, and JFT-300M for pre-training, and it reports transfer/evaluation results on ImageNet, ImageNet-Real, CIFAR-100, and the VTAB suite of 19 tasks. The evidence does not list any other datasets explicitly.

questions.question: What is the parameter count of the largest variant in 'Training data-efficient image transformers & distillation through attention'?
questions.gold_answer: The largest DeiT variant is DeiT-B★ (DeiT-B with distillation) at 87M parameters.
questions.system_answer: The largest variant listed is DeiT-B★, which has 87M parameters. The paper’s reference vision transformer is 86M, but the largest reported variant in the evidence is 87M.

questions.question: What architectural innovations does 'Swin Transformer: Hierarchical Vision Transformer using Shifted Windows' introduce?
questions.gold_answer: hierarchical structure, shifted window attention
questions.system_answer: The paper introduces a hierarchical vision Transformer that computes self-attention in non-overlapping local windows, then alternates with shifted window partitioning to create cross-window connections. It also uses patch merging to build multi-scale feature maps, and this design gives linear computational complexity with respect to image size. The evidence also notes the use of relative position bias, while absolute position embedding is reported to hurt dense prediction tasks.

questions.question: Briefly describe the architecture of 'Emerging Properties in Self-Supervised Vision Transformers'.
questions.gold_answer: DINO is a self-supervised vision transformer training method
questions.system_answer: The paper presents DINO, a simple self-supervised Vision Transformer architecture based on self-distillation without labels: a student network learns to match a momentum-teacher network across multiple augmented views, with centering and sharpening to prevent collapse. It is designed to work with both ViTs and convnets, but the paper highlights that ViTs with small patches benefit especially strongly.

questions.question: Do papers in this corpus agree on top-1 accuracy on ImageNet?
questions.gold_answer: No—reported top-1 ImageNet accuracy in this corpus varies widely from 1.3% to 91.0% across 594 results.
questions.system_answer: No. The corpus reports a very wide range of ImageNet top-1 accuracies, from 1.3 to 91.0 (spread 89.7, stddev 8.69), so the papers do not agree on a single value. There are also conflicting SOTA claims: CoCa (finetuned) is listed at 91.0, while other papers report lower claimed bests such as PaLI-17B at 90.9, MaxViT-XL at 88.7/88.51, and NFNet-F6+SAM or CaiT-M48†448† at 86.5.

questions.question: What is the range of top-1 accuracy values reported on CIFAR-100 in this corpus?
questions.gold_answer: The top-1 accuracy on CIFAR-100 in this corpus ranges from 48.5% to 91.88% across 27 results.
questions.system_answer: Across the corpus, reported CIFAR-100 top-1 accuracy values range from 48.5% to 91.88%, a spread of 43.38 percentage points. The highest value is 91.88% (AdaptFormer-Swin), and the evidence does not list any explicit SOTA claims for this dataset.

questions.question: Do papers in this corpus agree on which model holds SOTA on ADE20K mIoU?
questions.gold_answer: No—this corpus does not agree on a single ADE20K mIoU SOTA model, since 7 papers each claim SOTA on the benchmark.
questions.system_answer: No. The corpus does not agree on a single ADE20K mIoU SOTA model: one paper reports InternImage-H at 62.9 as the top value, while another paper claims ViT-Adapter-L at 61.5, and SwinV2-G is also listed at 59.9. The spread of reported results is large (26.6 to 62.9), so there are conflicting SOTA claims rather than consensus.

questions.question: Do papers in this corpus agree on mIoU values on ADE20K?
questions.gold_answer: No—reported ADE20K mIoU values vary widely across 97 results, from 26.6 to 62.9.
questions.system_answer: No. The reported ADE20K mIoU values vary widely in this corpus, from 26.6 to 62.9 (a 36.3-point spread, stddev 7.29), so they are not consistent. There are also conflicting SOTA claims: InternImage-H is listed at 62.9, while ViT-Adapter-L is listed at 61.5 and SwinV2-G at 59.9, indicating multiple papers claim top performance with different values.

questions.question: Do papers in this corpus agree on whether position embeddings are necessary for vision transformers?
questions.gold_answer: Papers disagree on the necessity of position embeddings; some ablations show minimal impact while others demonstrate clear performance drops without them.
questions.system_answer: No, the corpus does not agree. Some papers treat position embeddings as part of the standard ViT design, e.g. ViT says it “adds position embeddings,” while CPVT argues the usual learnable absolute positional encodings have problems and proposes conditional positional encodings instead. Other papers report that position embeddings can be removed or are less important in some settings: CvT says “Removing position embedding … does not degrade performance,” and the self-supervised ViT study says removing them “only slightly hurts performance.”

questions.question: How did the maximum reported top-1 accuracy on ImageNet change year over year in this corpus?
